metadata.title: Write Thought Leadership That Doesn't Feel Like Self-Promo
metadata.playlist: Build a Home — Thought Leadership on Substack
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 10 brief — Self-Promotion Through Thought Leadership

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 3 of 5. Part 2 of the assignment is the article — the piece that goes live on the Substack. Six hundred to eight hundred words. The article has to do three things at once: establish you as credible, introduce the tool naturally, and give the reader something genuinely useful. The moment the article fails any one of the three, it becomes something else. Fail the first and it is a student project. Fail the second and you miss ten points on the Madison Tool Integration line. Fail the third and it is an ad. This episode is about writing the piece that does all three. Let's go.

props.segment: Write Thought Leadership That Doesn't Feel Like Self-Promo
props.command: 600–800 words: establish credibility + introduce tool naturally + give reader something useful. Draw on A9 arc.
props.runningText: 3 simultaneous jobs — fail any one and it becomes something else; the skep plugin drafts, you own the insight…
output: Opening hook: first 2 sentences earn the 3rd
Framework or insight the reader keeps

beats.narration_text: The three simultaneous jobs. The first job is establishing you as credible. This does not mean writing a biography — it means demonstrating understanding of the problem, showing that you have been in the situation, and taking a position. A position is not 'AI is changing everything.' A position is 'most AI pipelines I have seen fail because the quality gate was written to check that the pipeline ran, not that the output was correct — and those are very different things.' That is a position. It implies expertise, it implies experience, and a reader in the field knows immediately whether to agree or disagree. The second job is introducing the tool naturally. Naturally means: the tool appears where it adds value to the argument, not where you want it to appear. 'The pipeline I built for this class — Madison, a verification-first AI digest tool — runs this quality check automatically and flags exceptions for the analyst before delivery' is natural. A paragraph that stops the argument to say 'by the way, I also built a tool called Madison' is an ad. The third job is giving the reader something to keep. A framework they can apply. An insight they will remember at three in the morning when their own pipeline is behaving strangely. A question that makes them look at their work differently. If you removed the part about you and the tool and the reader still got something from the piece, the third job is done.

props.artifactHeading: Credible · Tool natural · Reader keeps something
artifactLines: Job 1 — Credible: take a specific position. Not 'AI is changing everything' — a claim a peer can agree or disagree with.
Job 2 — Tool natural: tool appears where it adds value to the argument, not as an announcement.
Natural: 'the pipeline — Madison — runs this check automatically.' Ad: 'by the way I built a tool.'
Job 3 — Reader keeps something: a framework, an insight, a question that changes how they see their own work.
Test: remove the you-and-tool parts — does the reader still gain something? If yes, Job 3 is done.
props.sparkLine: Credible + natural + useful. Fail any one and it becomes something else.

beats.narration_text: The angle is the specific entry point into the topic. Two paths, same as A6 through A9. Path A — personal brand angles: your POV on a problem in your industry that most people are getting wrong; a lesson from your own work that others can apply to theirs; why the conventional approach to a thing is broken and what a better one looks like; how using AI in your work changed your practice in a way you did not expect. Path B — startup brand angles: the problem your tool was built to solve, told as a story from the user's perspective; a counterintuitive truth about the problem space that most solutions ignore; the framework your tool's design is built on and why it matters; what surprised you while building the tool that changed what you thought the problem was. The angle is not the thesis — it is the door. The thesis is the specific position you take after you walk through it. Do not start writing until the angle and the thesis are both clear. One sentence each: 'I am writing from the angle of [angle] and my thesis is [specific position].' If either sentence is vague, the article will be vague. The skep:take skill or the Your Turn prompt structures the draft from the A9 arc once the angle and thesis are set.

props.artifactHeading: Path A (personal) · Path B (startup) — angle + thesis
artifactLines: Path A: POV on problem others get wrong · lesson that applies to others · broken conventional approach · unexpected AI impact.
Path B: user's problem as a story · counterintuitive truth · design framework + why it matters · what building it revealed.
Angle = the door. Thesis = the specific position inside it.
Write both before drafting: 'Angle: [angle]. Thesis: [specific claim].'
Vague thesis → vague article. skep:take structures the draft once these are clear.
props.sparkLine: Angle is the door. Thesis is the position. Both in one sentence each — before you draft.

beats.act: the opening hook + the framework the reader keeps
beats.narration_text: The opening hook. The first two sentences earn the third. If a reader does not keep reading after the second sentence, the article does not exist for them. The best opening hooks for thought leadership do one of three things: they state the counterintuitive thing — 'The most dangerous AI pipeline is not the one that fails catastrophically. It is the one that runs every morning and nobody checks.' They open with a specific moment — 'Three weeks into the run, the daily digest was ranking a story about a sports team called the Tigers above every actual news story about supply-chain risk. The keyword was right. The output was wrong.' Or they ask the question the reader did not know they had — 'If your AI produced an output that was wrong in a subtle way, how long would it take you to notice?' After the hook, the body of the article needs to give the reader a framework or insight they can use. Not a summary of what you did — a transferable tool. Two to three steps. A labeled distinction. A question to check their own work. Something they can carry out of the article and apply before next week. Draw the A9 arc into the body: the turning point sentence from the A9 story is often the thesis of the thought-leadership article stated in narrative form.

props.artifactTitle: Opening hook + the framework they keep
artifactLines: Hook type 1: counterintuitive claim — 'The most dangerous AI pipeline is the one nobody checks.'
Hook type 2: specific moment — the situation, the problem visible only in hindsight.
Hook type 3: question the reader didn't know they had.
Body: give a transferable framework — 2–3 steps, a labeled distinction, a check question.
A9 turning point → thesis of the article stated in narrative form. Draw the arc in.
props.sparkLine: First 2 sentences earn the 3rd. Framework they keep earns the subscribe.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Write Thought Leadership. Three simultaneous jobs: credible — take a specific position a peer can agree or disagree with; natural — tool appears where it adds value to the argument, not as an announcement; useful — the reader takes something out they can apply. Choose the angle and write the thesis in one sentence each before drafting. The hook earns the third sentence; three hook types — counterintuitive, specific moment, or the question the reader did not know they had. The body builds around a transferable framework or insight. The A9 turning point sentence is often the thesis in narrative form. The skep:take skill or the Your Turn prompt structures the draft from that material. Episode 4 adds the headline, the visual, and the tool mention, then publishes it live.

props.artifactTitle: Write Thought Leadership That Doesn't Feel Like Self-Promo
artifactLines: 3 jobs: credible (specific position) · natural (tool adds to argument) · useful (reader takes something).
Before drafting: angle in 1 sentence + thesis in 1 sentence. Vague thesis = vague article.
Hook: counterintuitive · specific moment · question reader didn't know they had.
Body: transferable framework or insight. A9 turning point = thesis in narrative form.
skep:take → draft → voice pass. Episode 4 = headline + visual + publish.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The thought-leadership draft prompt. Paste this into Claude or into skep:take, fill in the brackets, and run it. The prompt reads: I am writing a thought-leadership article for my Substack, INFO 7375 Assignment 10. I am taking Path [A or B]. My angle is: [one sentence describing the entry point]. My thesis is: [one specific claim that a peer in my field could agree or disagree with]. My A9 turning point sentence is: [paste it]. The transferable framework or insight I want to give the reader is: [two to four bullet points — the steps, the distinction, or the check question]. My Madison tool reference: [one sentence describing what the tool does and where it naturally fits into the argument]. Draft a 600–800 word thought-leadership article with: an opening hook using [hook type: counterintuitive / specific moment / question]; the thesis visible in the first two paragraphs; the transferable framework or insight in the body, labeled and specific; the Madison tool mentioned naturally where it adds to the argument — not bolted on; subheadings every one hundred to one hundred fifty words; short paragraphs of two to four sentences; and a closing paragraph that names the one thing the reader can do with what they just read. After you get the draft, apply the voice pass from A9 Episode 3 — read every paragraph aloud and replace any sentence that could have come from a content algorithm.

props.segment: Write Thought Leadership That Doesn't Feel Like Self-Promo
props.command: Path [A/B]. Angle: [1 sentence]. Thesis: [specific claim]. A9 turning point: [paste]. Framework bullets: [2–4]. Tool mention: [1 sentence, natural placement]. Draft 600–800 words: hook type [choose] · thesis in first 2 paragraphs · labeled framework · tool natural · subheads · 2–4 sentence paragraphs · closing action.
props.runningText: voice pass after draft: read aloud, replace every sentence that sounds like a content algorithm…

beats.narration_text: Write Thought Leadership That Doesn't Feel Like Self-Promo. Liam, in for Bear.
